data.title: PHYS: Expect Some Near-Term Pressures, But Remain Focused On Long-Term Drivers
data.description:
Gold's spot price has been on a tear in recent months and with that the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:PHYS) has continued making new all-time highs.

The ETF achieved returns that are now comparable with those of the S&P 500 for the past 10-year period. What's quite unusual is that the equity market had one of its strongest years on record over that period and the supposedly lower risk - lower return precious metal has almost matched the cumulative annual growth rate of the equity index.

To illustrate that, PHYS annual return in the past 10 years stood at roughly 12%, which is above the long-term return profile of the equity market.

The precious metal bull market started all the way back in 2019, took a pause in 2021-22 period and has made an even stronger return after that. As one of the best ways to gain direct exposure to the price of gold, PHYS is up nearly 40% year-to-date and on track to achieve it best years on record.

Right of the bat, we have to establish a couple of characteristics about gold that some investors might be forgetting amidst this long-awaited bull market.

Firstly, the long-term thesis for gold is intact. I outlined it all the way back in 2019 and to put it briefly it relies on the need for a change of the current global monetary regime. Whether gradual or sudden, such change increases the attractiveness of monetary alternative like gold.

Secondly, keeping in mind everything said above, we should recognize that these are long-term trends. They do not occur over 1, 2 or 5 years. They could span over a decade or even more. That is why, short-term deviations from the long-term trend are quite common, especially when market participants are suddenly changing their minds about an asset class that has been shunned for many years.

Lastly, there is no way to accurately forecast the price of gold. As opposed to equities, where we could link the price to business fundamentals, the price of gold is a bit arbitrary. At one point most people believed that real interest rates were the best predictor of gold prices, but that narrative was quickly proven wrong in recent years.

The price of gold is no-longer related to real interest rates and the fears that higher rates would be a disaster for PHYS have been ill-informed.

Although predicting the price of gold is futile exercise, we could anticipate periods PHYS is likely to perform well and periods when the Trust's performance is likely to disappoint.

Using Leading Indicators As A Guidance

For this purpose we could take a look at the annual change of the Conference Board Leading Economic Index® (LEI). Since the Global Financial Crisis, there has been a tendency for either monetary or fiscal authorities to implement certain radical decisions when the annual change of the LEI turns negative and is signalling pending recession.

As we could see on the graph below, there are a number periods when the annual LEI change fell in negative territory to approached zero - 2012, 2016, 2019 and 2022 to today.

During the first period, in September of 2012, the Federal Reserve announced the beginning of its third round of quantitative easing, known as QE3. Thus enough liquidity was provided for a recession to be avoided. The price of gold did not react significantly to this event, but equities did.

The second period was in 2016 and around that time the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act was announced which provided enough support for the equity market. This time around, however, market participants begun to realize that monetary or fiscal intervention would probably be required every time a recession is on the horizon. Since this is a strong signal that the monetary regime is not working properly, the price of gold reacted strongly in early 2016 just to quickly cool-off by year end as risks subsided.

The next cycle begun in 2019, just prior to the pandemic. The yield curve begun steepening in mid-2019 and this sparked speculations that sooner or later more liquidity would be needed. This happened later on in 2020 when rates were cut and the Federal Reserve re-launched an aggressive quantitative easing program. This time around, however, it became crystal clear that the marked can not function properly without significant interventions from time to time and the gold price reacted accordingly.

The current downturn for LEI begun around 2022 and the risk of a recession has been exceptionally high for a number of years now. On top of the deteriorating leading indicators, we have also seen a relentless steepening of the yield curve

On top of all that, one of the biggest risks for the current monetary regime has been the growing fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP. What's likely sparking most fear among market participants is the fact that budget deficit tends to widen significantly during recessions, but for the first time since World War 2 we are witnessing such high deficits during period of economic growth.

In my view, this has pushed the risk of a major change in the current monetary regime to all-time high and that is why we are witnessing the strong performance of PHYS over the past few years. The 110% return in just 3 years, however, is significantly higher than any performance we have seen recently which creates the necessary conditions for a temporary setback in gold prices.

Additionally, the risk of a market sell-off is now extremely high and such an event would trigger a negative reaction of PHYS as usually the initial reaction to market participants is to rush and sell all assets.

Conclusion

The long-term attractiveness of PHYS as an addition to an all-equity portfolio remains intact. Until the current monetary regime is restructured or an entirely new system is put in place, gold would remain a good way to reduce portfolio volatility and hedge some major risks for equities. By closely following the annual changes of LEI and its short-term cycles, investors could distinguish between periods when adding gold is likely to pay-off and when the most prudent thing to would be just hold. With that in mind, I believe that right now the risk of a short-term correction of PHYS is too high and a Hold rating is justified.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Red Flag, Green Flag
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Oracle jumps to a new high on earnings, up 85% YTD (0:30). Adobe down - is monetization keeping pace? (1:35) Broadcom, Nebius surge on customer acquisitions (3:00). LULU drops post-earnings; retail and tariffs (3:55). Jobs data weaker than thought (8:50). IPO market waking up (11:00). Momentous Fed meeting (14:55).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Welcome to another week of Wall Street Roundup, by popular request now being recorded on a Friday morning after the jobs data.

Welcome to a Friday of Wall Street Roundup, Brian Stewart.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here. End of the week.

RS: End of the week. Always great to have you talking news even if it looks terrible outside our windows, spiritually speaking. Now what do you have on the agenda? We've had some tech earnings. We've had a bit of retail earnings. What are you mostly focused on as we say goodbye to another week?

BS: If we're looking at this week, I think the top story has to be Oracle (ORCL). The company jumped 76% to a new high following its earnings release.

It actually missed expectations for both earnings and revenue, but revealed four new major contracts. This represented a 359% surge in its remaining performance obligations, which are now sitting at about $455,000,000,000.

So the bet that a lot of people are making is just that the company's future looks really bright even if the last quarter was a bit of a meh.

The money that's been committed in terms of contract will eventually hit the bottom line, and so the company is going to see significant upside in the future.

RS: And what else would you add to the earnings conversation?

BS: I think Oracle is an interesting contrast to Adobe (ADBE). Adobe is down slightly now, after its earnings reported last night.

It had sort of the opposite situation. So it had a beaten raise in its most recent quarter. On the face of it, more impressive than Oracle, but the stock is just trailing off slightly.

Digging into the results, AI adoption has been pretty strong within the company, but there's some questions that analysts have about the extent to which the company has been able to monetize that.

So it's become sort of an interesting component within the company, but there's no take off of revenue generation.

Just as an example, the digital media unit in that company showed 11.6% growth in Q3. It had 11.3% in Q2. And then that growth had peaked in the most recent cycle at 12.6% back in 2023. So you're looking over the course of a few years. You see steady, relatively impressive growth, but nothing like Nvidia (NVDA) or even in what being is being promised for Oracle.

So it's just not as inviting a situation for investors who I think at this point are looking for home runs in the AI space.

RS: And what is happening in the AI tech space? We also saw Broadcom (AVGO) report. What would you say is the net effect across the board on, you mentioned NVIDIA, but the net effect on AI stocks and tech stocks in general, what's the general consensus, or what's part of the general consensus right now?

BS: You mentioned Broadcom, and I think that's an interesting stock to bring into it. So they reported last Friday, surged 9% following its earnings.

They beat expectations as well. Also announced new large customer, $10,000,000,000 customer for their AI chips, customized AI chips. There's a lot of speculation that customer is OpenAI, though neither company has confirmed that.

I think if you look at Oracle, which got a big pop based on contracts that it announced, people ignoring its recent earnings and looking more towards those customer acquisitions.

Meanwhile, you see Nebius (NBIS), they popped 49% on a deal that they signed with Microsoft (MSFT). That one, they're providing AI infrastructure at 17,400,000,000 over a five year span. So that's another company that got a huge surge just based on a customer acquisition.

You see the forward looking, it's not even in the guidance anymore, it's in these contract wins that the companies are getting. Broadcom also received a pop after the Oracle news, so there was just sort of a general AI pop there.

This was on Wednesday, Oracle rose following its earnings, and you saw Broadcom rise about 9% at the same time. You also saw Nvidia up about 4% on that day, and Palantir (PLTR) was up about 3%.

I think you're getting a small cadre of what people are considering the central AI growth stocks, and those are starting to react to each other as if they were a single sector.

RS: And as long as we're generally in the earnings conversation, Lululemon (LULU). I like to be a little bit affected when I say their name. They had earnings, and they saw a drop. What are the reasons, would you say, tariff based? What's happening over there?

BS: We didn't get to talk about it last week, but they reported on Friday and dropped 19% following their earnings.

They cut guidance basically based on tariffs. They pointed out that the elimination of the de minimis tariff exemption really hit their business hard. Analysts saying that this wasn't really baked into the stock.

The company also was pretty blunt about assessing their own situation. They called their product offering stale. So I think as a single stock story, they're taking a broad look at their offerings and the pricings and reassessing.

So there's a real turnaround situation trying to be put in play there. I think if you pull the lens back a little bit, and look at retailing in general, we saw some previous retail stocks get hit pretty hard on tariff related concerns.

You do have a situation now where these individual stocks are starting to feel the pinch of the new, international regime, and there's definitely gonna be winners and losers in the retail space from that.

RS: For stocks like Lululemon, on the higher price of things, are those more affected? We've been talking about these low cost retailers and how they're doing in the face of tariffs, in the face of economic hardship. What would you say to that point?

BS: I don't know if higher end is gonna feel the pinch because it's more expensive. We saw this play out with Dollar Tree (DLTR) was down sharply after its earnings largely because it's selling things so cheap that a relatively small increase dollar wise in the cost of what it's buying forces it to do a relatively high percentage change in the pricing.

So it's really challenging Dollar Tree's entire idea of itself, its business plan is based on getting the stuff extremely cheap. And so if that becomes 30%, 40%, 50% more expensive, it really matters to Dollar Tree a lot.

Meanwhile, we've seen some higher end retailers. Macy's (M) said their Bloomingdale's segment has done really well. And so that might be a consumer base that is immune to some of the shocks that we're seeing.

It could also be that because the items are already so high margin that the companies can afford to take a little bite and not really feel the pinch from that. So I think it's gonna be a little bit more complicated. I don't think there's gonna be a one to one situation.

I think investors, if you're looking into a retail stock, I think it's makes sense to look under the hood a little bit or try and find an ETF so that you're a little bit diversified as you you dip into the market.

RS: As investors, as participants in the market and in life, we really need to be thinking through things. They are not as obvious, to your point, it's not so simple, when the economy's bad, there's not necessarily a one to one correlation from sector to economy, from data to results. All these things, they must be picked apart.

BS: I think that's a good moral just for the current market. Disturbing economic data. We just got the job revisions for the twelve months leading to March, and that was revised down by 911,000 jobs, which basically represented half the jobs we thought were created during that period.

So every report about the jobs market, not only shows weakness as we stand now, but suggests that it has been weaker than we had expected even all along. Meanwhile, inflation data remains stubbornly high, roughly around 3%.

It looks like the Fed, which is meeting next week, is probably just going to give up on 2% inflation as a goal, and just declare victory at 3% and start cutting rates.

But even while this is happening, the market keeps finding new highs.

So you see, like you're pointing out, you would think that this succession of red flags for the economy would push the market lower, but the opposite is happening because I think that people are starting to concentrate, like I said, in these high growth possibilities.

So you see people ignoring an Adobe, but moving into an Oracle because it just looks like a a promising growth opportunity. So it'll be interesting how this plays out as we get more economic information. Maybe the rate cuts will be enough to sort of stem the economic tide.

Inflation won't shoot up due to tariffs or the falling interest rates. But, definitely stagflation is more of a concern than it had been. It's more of a reality. I mean, we're fundamentally in a stagflationary environment now this isn't like early eighties, late seventies kind of stagflation, but it's a mini stagflation as it stands. And so the question is, how long is it gonna last and how bad it's gonna go?

RS: And do not underestimate the psychology of the markets. Do not underestimate that factor in thinking about things.

To this point of what's happening in the world and the economy really segues nicely into - the IPO market is waking up a little bit and Klarna had its debut to the point of people needing to pay a bit later down the line because of economic situations. That's what Klarna (KLAR) is focused on. Talk to us a little bit about that. They saw a nice rise post debut.

BS: Yeah. That's interesting. I hadn't even really thought of that angle that Klarna might be in a good position in a more challenging economic environment. Then again, it survives on arbitraging rates in a certain sense.

So it might also run into difficulty as you start to see an uptick in delinquencies and things like that, they might feel the pinch there.

RS: Like we said, it's complicated.

BS: It's complicated. It rose 15% in its debut but finished off its highs. It's now trading below the highs that it reached immediately after going public.

There's been a run of higher profile IPOs that have done well lately. Bullish (BLSH) and Figma (FIG) come to mind.

Also, if you take things in aggregate, there's a a Renaissance IPO ETF. The ticker there is (IPO). It's up 20% year to date. It's meant to give broad exposure to recent IPOs. It's up 20% year to date, just off a fifty two week high.

So you see a lot of interests in these IPOs. It's a little top heavy and you get these higher profile ones, they get a lot of attention and get a little pop once they come up. If you look at a list of the IPOs, it's much more company to company. It's not like every IPO is is a winner.

The smaller ones tend to - it matters on the individual stock. But broadly speaking, you're seeing an interest there. So, if you're looking for a bullish factor, there's clearly demand for at least certain types of stocks. So there is investor money waiting to come into the market, just waiting for the right opportunity.

RS: Speaking of opportunities, we were talking on Investing Experts this week about small caps. Anything to say there?

BS: Traditionally, the small caps are kind of the leading edge of the market. They go up faster when the market goes up and go down faster when the market goes down. So it's a good leading indicator.

Even if you're not going to invest in those small caps, you can watch the action there as an indication of, it's just a much more volatile market than the S&P 500 (SP500) or the Dow (DJI) or whatever.

I think that in a troubled economic situation, smaller cap companies are more vulnerable. A company like NVIDIA, growth might fall off, but you're not gonna worry about NVIDIA going to zero.

And there could be, say, a small cap AI stock that seems like a big growth candidate. But if it runs into funding trouble, that might be it for the company.

So there's always a risk when you move down the market cap scale. It's a much more volatile situation. But on the other side, there's a much bigger chance for giant wins.

If I was giving advice to investors, I'd say, again, if you're betting on individual stocks, you really wanna dig under the hood in these conditions. I really don't think this is a dartboard market where you can just throw a dart at a bunch of names and whatever you get is gonna work out.

I really think it's getting to the point where it's a stock picker's market or you're gonna make collective bets in ETFs or in the broad market.

RS: And, of course, all advice, hypothetical, not literal on this podcast

BS: Yeah. I meant that educationally, not as a recommendation.

RS: We're thinking things through. Anything else for this week? What should we be looking for in the week ahead? Obviously, the Fed decision. What else would you add to that?

BS: Fed decision's obviously going to be the the central aspect. There was a 97% chance of a rate cut a week ago. Now that's 100% chance. And now the only question is whether it's going to be 25 basis points or 50 basis points. There's currently a 70% chance of a half point rate cut that was 0% a month ago.

So there's a feeling that the Fed might have missed its window a little bit and might have to catch up. You're also seeing an acceleration of rate cuts through the rest of the year.

So currently, there's an 80% chance that rates will be 75 basis points lower or more. By the end of the year, that chance was about fifty fifty a month ago. So you're looking for maybe three quarter point rate cuts at the three remaining meetings this year, or a half point cut this meeting or next meeting, and then a 25 basis point cut down the road a little bit.

So some combination in the next few meetings. That's what the market's baking in. There's also a 6% chance right now for a full percentage point lower by the end of the year.

That would be pretty dramatic, there'd be at least a half point rate cut and then two quarter point rate cut, something like that. So there's generally a feeling that's been growing in recent days that the Fed is gonna have to hit the gas on lowering interest rates.

Meanwhile, beyond the Fed meeting, there's not much in terms of earnings, but FedEx (FDX) is reporting next week. It's a good economic indicator stock that's central to a lot of companies' way they distribute their products, obviously, shipping packages.

And so even if you're not an investor or a potential investor in FedEx, it's always good to peek in on those earnings and see what it portends for the overall economy.

RS: In terms of the importance of the Fed meeting, it's getting a lot of press and, there's always talk about Fed meetings, but this one in particular, along with the previous one, feels very momentous.

What would you say as a fact man, as a newsman, what would you say in terms of contextualizing the importance of this Fed meeting?

BS: It has the the makings of a wild one, potentially. You still have the Lisa Cook debate, however you wanna frame that, going on. So it's not even clear at this moment whether she'll be a participant or not.

That kind of legal wrangling is ongoing. Whatever the decision is that the Fed makes at the meeting, you're likely gonna get dissenting votes. So it's gonna be a contentious meeting. It's gonna be a meeting with a lot of controversy.

There's gonna be eyes on what Trump's response to whatever the decision is going to be. There's also the usual kind of signaling that comes from the Fed either from their statement or from Powell's press conference.

So just in terms of possibilities, outcomes, this isn't your typical sleepy Fed meeting where everything's kinda wrapped up going into it and everyone's parsing placement of commas and things like that.

I think there'll be some actual news coming out of this meeting. So I agree with your assessment. This is going to be one of the most interesting meetings in a long time. I mean, probably since COVID calmed down.

We'll see what the commentary is vis a vis inflation and the economy. Because it's like I said, we are at mini stagflation right now, which is a kind of worst case economic scenario. So hearing Powell's recipe for getting out of that will be interesting to hear.

RS: We will obviously have coverage of the Fed meeting on Wall Street Breakfast, Wall Street Lunch. We're also going to have a a few dedicated episodes on Investing Experts about the Fed meeting.

Happy Friday, everybody. Happy Friday, Brian. Happy weekend, everybody. Don't forget to keep thinking things through, and don't forget to have real conversations with real people. Don't believe all the hyperbole on the Internet.

BS: Good advice.

RS: Talk it out.

data.title: Synopsys: Hurt By Design Issues
data.description:
I last took a look at shares of Synopsys, Inc. (NASDAQ:SNPS) in the spring of 2024, when I concluded that the company was selling assets on the cheap ahead of the Ansys deal closing.

At the time, the company sold its software integrity business in a $2 billion deal to pay for a $35 billion deal to acquire Ansys, as the divestment appeared to be taking place at cheap multiples. Such a cheap divestment ahead of a long and uncertain regulatory approval process provided added arguments to the cautious outlook for the shares.

With the deal finally taking place now, having closed over the summer, Synopsys sees a real slowdown, driven by market developments, but certainly also execution issues. While a violent pullback might be interesting, fundamental valuation support is still not yet seen.

Shedding Perspective

Early in 2024, Synopsys announced the purchase of Ansys to create a stronger, larger, and more diversified Electronic Design Automation market leader.

The company focuses on electronic design automation as well as design IP, both critical to accelerating electronics innovation, which stands at the forefront of foundry, semiconductor and electric system markets. This is the first step in the process, which eventually facilitates the rise of megatrends such as datacenters, networking, automotive, 5G, industrial automation, and others.

Over the past five years through 2023, revenues have risen some 70% to nearly $6 billion, with the company posting operating profits near $1.3 billion, resulting in GAAP earnings of $8 per share in 2023. Adjusted earnings came in at $11 per share, yet the vast majority of the $3 difference stems from stock-based compensation expenses, which I am not happy to adjust for.

A $550 stock was valued at $85 billion at the time, equal to 14 times sales and a very demanding earnings multiple at around 70 times GAAP earnings. All this changed as the company announced a $35 billion deal to acquire Ansys, to be paid roughly 50/50 between cash and stock. The transaction was designed to add silicon system design solutions capabilities to the business, creating a more holistic offering for its customers. The deal would add some $2.2 billion in sales, suggesting that a premium 15 times sales multiple has been paid.

This multiple was rather similar to the valuation of Synopsys, with operating margins being fairly similar as well. However, the company targeted some $400 million in cost savings (in year three post closing), instrumental in combination with the strategic rationale to offer a wider suite of solutions and to become a stronger business. That was needed as pro forma net debt would be seen around $17 billion, for a leverage ratio of around 4 times.

Given the discussion at the time, I was very cautious at $550 per share, certainly as the company sold its software integrity business in a $2.1 billion deal (including milestone payments). Following this transaction, some $525 million in sales would be leaving the door, and with the assets being valued around 4 times sales (at the best), this marked a huge discount to the overall valuation multiple.

Such cheaper divestments and an uncertain regulatory environment, together with premium valuations, made me cautious, despite an impeccable positioning of the business.

Coming Down

Since the spring of 2024, shares have been largely trading in a $400-600 range. In December of last year, the company reported its results for the fiscal year 2024, with revenues up 15% to $6.13 billion, as GAAP operating profits advanced further to $1.35 billion, with GAAP earnings from continuing operations moving up to $9.41 per share.

The company offered solid guidance for 2025 seeing sales up to a midpoint of $6.775 billion, with GAAP earnings seen around $10.50 per share, all this of course excluding the contribution of Ansys, seen closing in 2025.

At the start of this year, the company announced that it had reached an agreement to sell Ansys PowerArtist to Keysight Technologies, Inc. (KEYS) a deal that needed to be done in order to obtain regulatory approval, as few details were given.

In February, the company started the year with a soft set of first quarter numbers, with revenues down nearly 4%. This was on par with the result of divestments anticipating the Ansys deal closing. Second quarter results revealed a real return to growth, with revenues up 10% on the year before, as the deal for Ansys finally closed in July of this year, that is during the third quarter of the business.

A Bombshell Report

Given the uncertainty in relation to the pending deal, the third quarter results being released in September were highly anticipated. For the quarter ending July 31st, the company grew sales by 14% to $1.74 billion, with Ansys contributing about two weeks to the quarterly results.

Adjusted earnings for the quarter came in at $3.39 per share, down four cents compared to the year before. However, the adjustment for stock-based compensation rose to $1.66 per share, making the realistic earnings come in around $1.73 per share, down meaningfully compared to the same period last year.

In between retained earnings and divestments, net debt is reported at $11.7 billion, which is comforting as the business is seeing some pressure on margins here. This is not expected to improve anytime soon.

The company guided for fourth quarter sales between $2.23 and $2.26 billion, which feels a bit soft as it annualizes at $9 billion here. This suggests that growth is slowing down, attributed to softness in the design IP business, as adjusted earnings are seen moving down to a midpoint of $2.78 per share, with stock-based compensation likely being substantial going forward.

Falling earnings are also the result of dilution incurred given the stock component in the Ansys deal, with the quarterly share count seen up to 187 million shares, up from about 160 million shares ahead of the deal.

And Now?

Based on the dramatic shortfall in earnings seen, adjusted earnings trend closer to $11 per share instead of numbers closer to $15 per share in recent times. Moreover, this is ahead of stock-based compensation expenses which might reveal that realistic earnings are only seen around $5 per share here, based on recent stock-based compensation expenses. Of course, a $400 million synergy target could make a huge impact, potentially adding $2 per share to pre-tax earnings, but that still has to be realised, of course.

Subsequently, shares plunged, with shares falling 36%, down $216 per share. Including the to be issued shares, this suggests that $40 billion in value has gone up in smoke, a greater number than the valuation attached to Ansys at the time of the deal!

With a current $72 billion equity valuation and $83 billion enterprise valuation, expectations have come in quite a bit, with revenues trending around $9 billion on an annual basis, reducing sales multiples from 15 times in 2024 to 9 times here. That is a lot cheaper already but comes amidst slower growth and, moreover, highly pressured earnings here.

At best, the issue at hand is that of a rocky integration, as growth is slowing down while the two companies are being integrated. The true pace of growth is, furthermore, hard to measure. While one can normalize for the pro forma growth pending the Ansys deal, the company has recently made two divestments as well. Furthermore, third quarter results clearly indicated that the weakness is seen in the IP business, a segment that saw third quarter sales fall by nearly 8%.

This is driven by new export restrictions to China, challenges at a major foundry customer, as well as a roadmap and resource allocation decision that turned out to be wrong, or at best ill-advised.

Usually, I am attracted to such big pullbacks. In between growth rapidly slowing down, earnings being highly pressured, and real growth issues at hand, valuations are a long way from providing support, even after a 35% pullback.

This makes me very cautious here, as much remains unknown at still elevated expectations.

data.title: What's At Stake As U.S. Supreme Court Considers Tariff Case
data.description:
The fate of U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs now lies in the hands of the U.S. Supreme Court. Trump is asking the court to overturn a lower court ruling that most of his administration’s tariffs exceeded the president’s authority. Chris Krueger, Managing Director at TD Cowen Washington Research Group, discusses the different scenarios and the implications for Trump’s trade agenda.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: The fate of US President Donald Trump's tariffs now likely lies in the hands of the US Supreme Court. Trump is asking the court to overturn a lower court ruling that most of his tariffs imposed on countries like Canada are illegal. A lot at stake, including potentially having to pay billions in refunds.

Chris Krueger, Managing Director at TD Cowen Washington Research Group, has just come out with a report on what the next steps might be.

Chris, how are you?

Chris Krueger: I'm well. Great to be back with you, Kim.

Kim Parlee: Nice to have you back. I like to check in to see how you are, because you're in the business of following every move, and I worry about you, Chris. I want to make sure you're OK. [LAUGHS]

Chris Krueger: That's very kind, Kim. Thank you.

Kim Parlee: Let's talk about the tariffs. So, a lower court has said these are illegal. And now we go through the process to get it through to the Supreme Court. What are the possible scenarios that could come out of this? What are you looking at?

Chris Krueger: Yeah. Well, so the International Court said that IEEPA tariffs were illegal. The appellate court, in a 7-4 decision, upheld that. So, we're now waiting for the Supreme Court to weigh in. Supreme Court is 9 justices. It breaks down 6-3 in terms of Republican to Democratic appointees.

Big, big, big picture, tariffs will remain. The issue is the type of tariff the statute involved. IEEPA has never been used for tariffs before. The term "tariff" is not in that statute. The administration is preparing a Plan B with Section 338 that gives them the ability to tariff up to 50%.

The issue here, which you correctly addressed upfront, was the potential for refunds. This would really punch a hole in the Treasury. One of the more surreal twists of the year has been that the bond market vigilantes are now very much in favor of tariffs. So there's a concern within the bond market that if the IEEPA tariffs have to be refunded, yields will spike, stocks might sell off, because you do punch a hole in the Treasury, at least $100 billion. We'll see what the Supreme Court comes up with. Our expectation is that they will take the case. It may be until next summer, though, Kim.

Kim Parlee: That's-- I mean, that's the surprising thing I saw in your note, was that how long it will take them to hear arguments and make a decision. So, you kind of wonder what the bond market and everyone else is going to do in the meantime.

Chris Krueger: Well, and each month, that tariff revenue really increases. If we have to wait until next summer, that refund check may not be in the billions. It might have a T in front of it.

Kim Parlee: Can I ask-- the one thing you just said that was interesting, that you said that the tariffs will remain, despite the decision, from your perspective, but the statute to which they're attached to may change. But is it also-- for lack of a better word, the height of the tariffs? I mean, the amount that could be tariffed through IEEPA was, I thought, much higher than in other ways.

Chris Krueger: Right now, the cap is-- both Brazil and India have 50% tariffs. Section 338 from the Tariff Act of 1930s, which is Smoot-Hawley, has a ceiling of 50. In particular for listeners in Canada, too, the Section 232 tariffs are not being litigated. So those stay regardless. And that is a disproportionate impact on Canada. That's 50% steel, aluminum, copper, and then the 25% on autos and auto parts. That is not being litigated, and those stay regardless.

Kim Parlee: Talk to me a bit about the transshipment tariffs and just-- you have a piece here talking about the new definition and rules of origin with regards to Chinese content. What are you referring to there?

Chris Krueger: Right. So, broadly speaking and within the USMCA renegotiation, this was going to be an issue if Kamala Harris had won. This is a big bipartisan issue. Candidly, it's more of a Mexico issue than a Canada or US issue.

But transshipment, it's a legal term. It's basically moving goods through a country, maybe changing that product a little bit and saying it's assembled in Mexico or assembled in Canada, when in reality that product is Chinese in nature. Transshipment is used to evade tariffs.

So, part of those Liberation Day tariffs-- really the new one that jumped out at us-- was this idea of a transshipment tariff. This would be 40%. It's stacking, so it's on top of existing tariffs. And the idea is, essentially, if you, as an importer/exporter, if you are found in violation of the transshipment rules, that product will have an additional 40%.

There's some thought that, within the broader USMCA renegotiation, a way to effectively end transshipment would be for Canada and Mexico to match US tariffs on China. Canada actually did this last summer when then President Biden issued a number of tariffs on Chinese EVs.

So there is precedence for it. It would be sort of an elegant solution. And the end game, I think, would be pretty positive for the entire continent. It's sort of this idea of a factory North America, a fortress North America-- granted, with high tariff walls against China. But within the continent, it could have some really interesting-- sort of a manufacturing renaissance, if you will.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, it really would. It would make a huge difference to Canada and Mexico too. Let me ask you-- I've only got about 45 seconds, Chris. But when you think about USMCA, as we're going forward, what are you expecting to have happen based on what you know today?

Chris Krueger: There will be renegotiation. I'm not sure there's ever going to be a big bow attached to a trade deal and then we can all just sort of get a good night's sleep. I think it's going to be-- it's going to take a long time. There also might-- it might be very incremental.

If you look at South Korea, they had a free trade agreement with KORUS. But then they've also done a reciprocal deal. So I think they'll probably start with some of the 232 relief. And then we'll go on to some of the trickier issues, like rules of origin, labor, dairy, transshipment, and others.

Original Post

data.title: Genmab: Epkinly's Spectacular Data Is The Antidote To The Darzalex Patent Cliff
data.description:
Introduction

Best known for its royalty-centric model, Genmab A/S (GMAB) is hoping that its ADCs, namely epcoritamab, can breathe life into the company before royalty growth normalizes.

Alongside its partner, AbbVie (ABBV), Genmab announced Phase 3 (P3) data for epcoritamab in follicular lymphoma (FL) in early August. Genmab’s stock has since rallied considerably. So, what gives?

The following article dissects Genmab’s ADC strategy and epcoritamab’s potential in the crowded liquid tumor market, which I believe was materially boosted following P3 FL data.

Genmab's Current Business & Epkinly Data

Let’s begin by discussing where Genmab is today before getting into the nitty-gritty of P3 FL data.

Genmab generated $925 million in its second quarter of 2025. Its EPS was $0.54. Both topline numbers bested consensus estimates. Net profit was $336 million. Genmab’s profits are spectacular. For instance, in the trailing twelve months, its gross profit margin is nearly 95%, and this efficiency trickles all the way down to its bottom line. This is a function of its royalty-centric model. Genmab outlicenses the drugs it develops and reaps the benefit should they succeed without having to pay costs like manufacturing, marketing, etc. The drawback of this is that Genmab’s revenues are capped. It can only make a fraction of a drug’s TAM.

Within this model, Genmab’s current “darling” is Darzalex (partnered with J&J). This is a popular antibody for multiple myeloma. Darzalex's global net sales were $6.776 billion in the first six months of 2025, up 22% YoY. Genmab’s royalty share totaled $1.378 billion during H1. Darzalex's momentum is accelerating, as evidenced by its royalties being $789 million in Q2 alone. Critically, Genmab’s commercialized portfolio saw 60% YoY growth to $289 million in Q2.

A pressing issue is Darzalex's upcoming patent expiration. The blockbuster’s US composition of matter patent expires in 2029. However, the company has explicitly expressed that their Darzalex royalties “will begin to decline materially in 2029” due to territorial patent tail, royalty mechanics (that accelerate erosion), and arbitration outcomes (high SC mix ≠ longer Genmab royalty life).

Moving on, Genmab also earns revenue from Novartis’ (NVS) KESIMPTA, a multiple sclerosis treatment. KESIMPTA’s patents aren’t as cut-and-dry as Darzalex's. Originally approved way back in 2009, this one is just riding dosing/method patents into the late 2030s, absent successful challenges.

Genmab recognizes royalties on a couple of other assets, but KESIMPTA and Darzalex are clearly its bread and butter.

(Another Seeking Alpha analyst did a better job than me outlining all of Genmab’s collaborations with specifics on revenue shares, etc. I highly recommend giving the April 2025 Genmab article a read.)

Elsewhere, Genmab markets TIVDAK (partnered with Pfizer/Seagen), an ADC for cervical cancer, and Epkinly (epcoritamab; partnered with AbbVie), a bispecific antibody for B-cell lymphoma.

For TIVDAK, Pfizer books U.S. net sales and profits are split 50:50. Global TIVDAK sales in H1 were $78 million. This one was fully approved in April 2024 and is primarily used as a second-line therapy in recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer. Per GlobalData, TIVDAK is projected to generate over $700 million in sales by 2029.

Epkinly Market Opportunity

For Epkinly, Genmab books net product sales in the U.S. and Japan, and AbbVie co-promotes. The revenue share in the U.S. is 50:50 pre-tax profit between the two. The FDA granted accelerated approval for this asset last year for “adult patients with relapsed or refractory [FL] after two or more lines of systemic therapy.” Now, the P3 data I alluded to earlier hopes to expand Epkinly’s label into earlier-line therapy. The company submitted an sBLA for Epkinly in combination with R+L (rituximab + lenalidomide) and was granted priority review. Approval is anticipated later this year.

For context, R+L is one of the few preferred treatment options for symptomatic patients with higher tumor burden. The results of EPCORE FL-1 are actually pretty remarkable. It compared Epkinly+R+L to R+L. The triplet “experienced a 79% reduction in the risk of death or disease progression compared with those treated with only rituximab and lenalidomide (HR, 0.21; P < .0001).” This effect size is bound to change guidelines and prescriber behavior, especially as R+L is already familiar.

If approved, the triplet would be the first bispecific combo in 2L FL. The competitive setup is favorable. CAR-T (e.g., KYMRIAH and YESCARTA) is generally utilized after two lines due to logistics and toxicity. LUNSUMIO is another bispecific antibody, but this one is used in 3L.

With this in mind, let’s model Epkinly in 2L FL.

There are ~15,000 U.S. FL incidents/year, and two-thirds of patients ultimately receive at least 2 lines. Time to 2L, however, can take a few years, so annual 2L starts must reflect both new incidence and prevalent relapses. A good working range, therefore, is ~6-9k US 2L starts/year. I’ll settle for 7k. The public WAC for Epkinly is ~$30k/month. However, earlier lines cite $37.5k/month and ~9 months on average on treatment. So, per-patient drug revenue is $270-$360k before discounts/discontinuations.

So, this is pretty close to a blockbuster opportunity. My model implies that Epkinly in 2L FL represents ~$1 billion in NPV (50:50 share) for Genmab. It’s very possible that Epkinly's uptake overperforms my estimates, which are purposely conservative. Reasons for this include a clear efficacy delta versus the entrenched comparator that is widely used in 2L. Moreover, R+L are generic/biosimilar, so there will be a lower combo cost share. Payers will be more likely to focus on the value of the Epkinly increment.

In any event, Epkinly validates Genmab’s “co-promotion” strategy and effectively helps offset the upcoming Darzalex patent expiry.

Rina-S Analysis

Rinatabart sesutecan, or Rina-S, is inching closer to the ovarian cancer market. This is likely Genmab’s best solid-tumor shot. After promising data in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer, or PROC, it is now in a pivotal H2H versus chemo (RAINFOL-02), with data expected next year. Rina-S attempts to improve upon ELAHERE (which is expected to be a blockbuster drug) via escaping the “FRα-high gate” and avoiding signals of ocular toxicity. AbbVie’s ELAHERE owns FRα-high PROC after the MIRASOL OS/PFS wins. However, the RAINFOL-02 trial stipulates that ELAHERE-eligible patients are expected to get ELAHERE first, so a win in RAINFOL-02 would be unlikely to automatically grant a FRα-high-naïve label. In addition, other companies are pursuing FRα in this population.

Valuation

Despite GMAB’s recent rally (+30% in the past month), it’s hard to argue that it is close to being overvalued. Its P/E GAAP (forward) of 16.53 trades around a 33% discount to its peers in the Healthcare sector. Typically, when this happens, it is because a company’s growth or profitability is inferior. However, this is far from being true for Genmab, as I’ve discussed earlier. It’s possible that the market was quite pessimistic on Genmab’s ability to diversify revenue beyond small royalties, especially ahead of Darzalex's patent expiry, and that Epkinly's spectacular P3 FL data put those concerns to rest.

GMAB appears undervalued on a cash flow basis as well.

As for inputs, I went with 7.5% YoY revenue growth (on average, over the nine-year projection), 25% FCF margin (2025 guide implies ~32% operating margin at midpoint; however, this is expected to compress as the mix shifts from royalties to profit-share), and stock beta 0.9 (blended). Everything else is either standard methodology (e.g., terminal growth % 2.25) or copy/paste (e.g., 10-year treasuries (US10Y), or risk-free rate, is 4.04%).

According to my Monte Carlo simulation, which accounts for variability in key inputs, GMAB’s price falls below the 5th percentile.

This implies that the market is far more cautious than my estimates, begging the question: why is the market discounting GMAB? Well, many are expecting Darzalex to flip from >20% growth to low single digits by 2027-2028 before “falling off a cliff.” Genmab remains predominantly dependent on royalties. Epkinly P3 FL was stellar, but the CD20xCD3 field is crowded. It's possible Epkinly sees more friction as it enters earlier lines (think CRS management, step-up visits). Moreover, moving to an “own-more” strategy will drag down Genmab’s margins. So, growth has to be compelling to overcome this. Bears are probably penciling in more conservative FCF margins (~20-21%). Outside of hematology, there is warranted pipeline skepticism. Solid-tumor ADCs are crowded.

So, although I am more optimistic than the market, the “base” (what is implied by GMAB’s current valuation) and “bear” cases should be respected. What could flip the narrative? Epkinly execution and fast 2L FL uptake, royalty durability clarity with controlled erosion path, cash discipline (small bolt-ons > trophy assets), and ADC proof at scale.

The Bottom Line

Genmab is “firing on all cylinders.” Its royalty business is stronger than ever, with considerable headway still to be made. Critically, Epkinly has become a major player in FL, likely supporting blockbuster potential and validating Genmab’s ability to score revenue beyond mere ~10% royalties. Meanwhile, Genmab’s stock is undervalued, and the investment narrative is gradually shifting (from capped royalties to profit share). Genmab’s $3 billion in cash (and counting) does give it some optionality.

On the other hand, I would like to see the company do a better job of highlighting royalty durability, and GMAB’s story may hinge on inherently uncertain Rina-S outcomes in the next year, which may be cause for pause (the bar is high). Overall, there’s a lot to like about GMAB (initiated with a Buy rating), but this is no free lunch.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oklo's Superb Rally Sparks Caution And Downgrade
data.description:
Oklo Inc. (NYSE:OKLO) has rallied almost exactly by 200% since my buy call in late April 2025. The core thesis of Oklo's microreactors addressing the clean energy needs of AI data centers still remains intact. However, richer valuations, now without commensurate progress on revenue timelines, cash burn, and dilution risks, make the case for a downgrade to Hold. This has a lot to do with profit-taking after a substantial rally on the core thesis. I believe re-entries should be on investors' radars after either better valuations or more meaningful progress.

A Review of the Core Thesis

The core thesis in April was a combination of three important factors. The core vision, operational progress, and valuations. Overall, I had found the vision compelling enough at the prevalent valuations back then to ignore operational progress details. The valuations have changed, so that brings into focus the operational details. The core vision's fundamentals mostly stay intact. The TAM, for instance, has not changed—AI-driven demand is potentially a multi-decade tailwind.

Valuations Have Turned Expensive

One of the gauges of valuations I had used in my buy thesis earlier was the relative market cap of Oklo versus NuScale, which had favorably retracted to levels that acknowledged NuScale's greater proximity to deployment (despite deployment setbacks). The relative progress still remains where it was broadly, but Oklo's market cap of $10.89b has now jumped over NuScale's $9.77b. The price ratio has also turned against Oklo's earlier relatively attractive entry points that lasted well into July, even as Oklo shares doubled in the period. After July, Oklo's next leg of rally, from $50 to $75 levels, has left NuScale behind and calls for caution in the absence of progress indicators or revenue-based valuation comparisons. The Price to Book Value ratio has also now jumped from ~10x at the time of my buy call to ~15x now, suggestive of speculative excesses.

Other Factors

Some of the factors that were not a concern at the earlier relatively conducive valuations should now come to the fore as pressures on a further rally. I had already highlighted how microreactors are not a new concept on their own and how Oklo's AI positioning helps its narrative. It is not immune to competition, notably from larger players like Constellation. Competition is likely to keep Oklo's pricing power at bay, even if it makes inroads. Critical differentiators in terms of quicker deployments due to smaller-sized units are a possibility. So is modular deployment compared to existing nuclear power operators, leading to better efficiencies. Oklo could also offer better economics for mid-sized customers in terms of lower upfront capex and faster payback. However, all such possibilities will need a proof of concept, which is too early to be evaluated until an initial few deployments and feedback are in place.

Additionally, Oklo's operating losses have been widening, which is not a critical concern given its early-stage profile but weighs on the stock at higher valuations. EPS numbers in Q2 2025 came out $0.07 worse than street expectations as well.

Dilution risks have also not shown signs of abatement in the past few months. In June, Oklo announced a further dilution by raising ~$400m by issuing 6.67m new shares. In the past year alone, the number of shares outstanding has increased by a material 20%. Given the dive in operating income and capital needs at this stage, further dilution is a very real threat to existing shareholders.

Momentum May Continue, but Exercise Caution

Much of the drag on the financials and valuation pressures is relative. For instance, the cash burn guidance issued in Q1, of $65-80m for the full year, has been indicated as on track in Q2. With a cash buffer of ~$225m and an additional buffer of marketable securities worth ~$450m, the cash burn does not seem to be an immediate cause for concern. Given the stage of the company, cash burn, operating income, and even EPS misses are not likely to be material impacts on share prices. Share prices are now reacting only to the narrative, which is still strong. A strong momentum may offset the concerns around the financial aspects I have highlighted. But progress indicators should still be of real value and influence the narrative-led share price rally.

In the last few months, Oklo has not shown tremendous progress on deployment proximity too, and that is real pressure on the valuations, if not financials. Despite positive NRC readiness assessments, a lack of relevant licenses still pushes back deployments potentially years away. This is more important given that competitors like NuScale and TerraPower have shown closer to commercial milestones. Oklo is expected to submit its Combined License Application sometime this year. With anywhere between 2-3 years required for a typical NRC review, the earliest deployment date seems to be sometime in late 2027 to early 2028 at best.

The 14 GW pipeline is encouraging, but a chunk of those agreements are non-binding Master Power Agreements. The DoE has also selected Oklo for 3 of its 11 advanced reactor pilot projects, but even there are multiple competitors and milestone-based decision-making involved.

Overall, as I have said a couple of times before, these deployment progress indicators are potentially promising, although nothing concrete is visible to date. That is probably okay for the stage of the company. What makes the stock more answerable to better indicators, both financially as well as in terms of deployment progress, is the valuations. With revenue estimates still insignificant by 2027 (~$14m), the Price to Sales Ratio is now at ~750x, even accounting for revenues 2-3 years down the line. Even if Oklo manages to reach revenues of ~$500m in the next 5 years, the P/S ratio works out to be ~20x—still excessive for a capital-intensive utility company.

Of course, Oklo is positioned as more than just a utilities company, and that narrative could keep demanding higher re-ratings over time. My call is now a hold, with a view to book partial profits while keeping some position alive on the narrative's momentum, which is real. The downgrade is a combination of higher valuations and dilution risks, while a similar level of execution risks still persists from where we were a few months ago (the pipeline and a positive NRC readiness assessment still are behind competition and present similar execution risks, given the magnitude of the hurdles ahead compared to the milestones achieved). I will await either a valuation reset or progress on the Combined License Application or more binding deals with hyperscalers before re-initiating fresh entries.

data.title: What Looms Ahead After The Dow Jones Topped A Record Of 46,000
data.description:
On Thursday, September 11, 2025, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) reached a new high of 46,000. Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ) and S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) also closed at all-time highs. The mainstream media attributed the performance of those indices to expectations of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates. The Consumer Price Index sped up in August, while core CPI held steady.

Some readers might express skepticism about the relevance of the Dow Jones, since it is a price-weighted index. Still, a record close at 46,108 on Thursday is a milestone worth celebrating. A company with a higher share price will have a bigger impact on the index’s movement than one with a lower price. However, the Dow Divisor will adjust stock splits, dividends, and other related corporate actions for shareholders. In that regard, I will comment first on stocks with the highest share price on the Dow.

Investors have three things to consider. What does this mean for investors for the rest of this year and into 2026? Which companies and sectors are fueling the Dow Jones’ surge? I explored the data in various tables. What should investors do, from both a long-term and short-term perspective?

Dow Jones Holdings

When sorted by allocation, Goldman Sachs accounted for over 10% in weighting on the Dow Jones (NYSEARCA:DIA) ETF. The financial institution benefited from increasing its market share in Banking and Markets by 370 basis points. Chief Executive Officer David Solomon said that revenue increased from the mid-$30 billion range to around $55 billion last year. Assets under management increased by 77%, compared to 2019 levels.

In the table below are the Dow components with a weighting of nearly 5% or more. They contributed meaningfully to the Dow’s surge this year.

What It Means for Investors in 2025-26

For the rest of this year and into 2026, valuation risks in shares of Boeing, 3M, Amazon, JPMorgan Chase, Goldman, and Nvidia may limit the Dow’s performance. They have a valuation score as low as an F and as high as a D. All of the companies except for Amazon have strong momentum scores. They have varied growth and profitability grades. This suggests that their YTD returns depended on bullish investor sentiment.

The DJIA will require continued outperformance in AI suppliers. Expect accelerated growth in both Microsoft and Nvidia to support the index. However, investors may hold a Nasdaq or S&P 500 ETF to gain higher exposure to the sector.

The DJIA is attractive for investors who want to bet on six companies rebounding from a YTD loss.

Since a judge ruled on a pause in a change to the Affordable Care Act, shares of UnitedHealth rose from $307.42 to $359.74 recently.

Drug firm Merck would benefit if pressure on firms to lower drug prices in the U.S. within a few weeks does not pan out. Readers may consider non-Dow components like AbbVie (ABBV), GSK (GSK), Novo Nordisk (NVO), or Sanofi (SNY). However, compared to the other firms, Merck leads in the industry rank at 16 out of 174. Xeris (XERS) scores first in that category.

Salesforce risks performing poorly for the rest of the year. Analysts believe that AI is not helping with its guidance just yet.

Profit margin pressure on consumer goods will likely continue. The Producer Price Index fell while the CPI rose. This suggests that sellers absorbed tariff costs for now. They likely cut jobs to offset the higher expenses. As tariffs raise import costs, they would need to pass them on to consumers. That would hurt P&G’s sales, as consumers find cheaper alternatives to fabric and home care, baby care, grooming, and personal health care products.

What Investors Should Do

Readers with a short-term perspective should consider actively trading companies in the technology sector. Companies collectively spent at least a trillion dollars on AI. Customers building AI have yet to realize a positive return on their investment. Reuters reported that OpenAI expects to burn $115 billion through 2029. Still, the AI firm will spend $300 billion for Oracle’s (ORCL) compute power.

In the long term, the DJIA offers a diversified exposure to the major sectors. Companies in those sectors achieved a positive average return, except for healthcare. For UNH, the star ratings lifted the stock. This uptrend should continue through 2026.

Your Takeaway

The Dow Jones achieved a 46,000 milestone. While it may risk a mild pullback as investors take profits, the index holds solid companies that should continue to perform well.

The Federal Reserve’s meeting next week is another short-term event. Investors will need to see the central bank’s statement on inflation and jobs. More importantly, investors will want to see if the Fed plans to cut rates again after September.

data.title: EEM: Understanding The Original Emerging Market Equity ETF
data.description:
What is an "Emerging Market" anyway? Investors have likely heard the term at some point. But when conducting research for this article, I found a dearth of published material that was not focused on current holdings, performance and other timely aspects of investing in emerging market equity ETFs like the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (NYSEARCA:EEM).

But I've invested in ETFs like this one since it debuted in 2003. So I'll fill in some lack of deep research with some personal experiences with this ETF. Because while it is the original "EM" ETF, there are some nuances about investing in this part of the global economy that are important for any investor to consider.

By way of background, EEM was created by BlackRock via its market-leading iShares unit. It owns many more stocks than would be considered sufficiently diversified. Typically it holds more than 1,000 of them. It tracks the world's primary emerging markets index, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. That index was formed by Morgan Stanley in 1988. I am old enough to have spent a couple of years at that firm's NYC headquarters, shortly after that index was launched, and remember speaking to the original index producers at the time. Little did I know what would become of it in the ETF era, which began in 1993, my first year there.

Back then, emerging markets were primarily focused on Brazil, South Africa and Mexico. However, not long after that, around the turn of the century, Asia began to rise to prominence. That included a then-nascent Chinese equity market that had not directly opened up to US investors, as well as South Korea, Indonesia, and several other smaller but emerging economies.

As the 21st century continued, markets emerged and even merged. But China's equity market, mostly traded through exchanges in Hong Kong and Taiwan, became the biggest player in EM. By doing so, it followed the lead of its neighbor Japan, which as of 1970 was a stock market in its infancy. After all, this was just 25 years after WWII, Pearl Harbor, D-Day, and Hiroshima/Nagasaki. Japan is now a well-developed nation. However, in some ways, its path to that, which truly took shape during the 1990s. Again, from my experience, my first job out of college was with a Japanese bank in NYC's World Trade Center, where I saw first-hand how such firms were gaining prominence and influence in the global economy.

But Japan left the ranks of "emerging" a while back, and South Africa too has essentially "emerged." China went on to become one of the biggest global equity markets, yet continued to be classified as emerging for decades.

EM, and thus EEM the ETF, are intended to offer investors not only access to an ever-increasing range of developing economies but also certain growth characteristics to act alongside the domestic equity exposure of investors in the developed markets. Emerging countries are assumed to have faster economic growth, similar to how smaller companies can grow revenue at faster percentage rates in their early stages of existence.

The other appeal is essentially a self-fulfilling prophecy that has persisted throughout the history of EM investing. Investors tend to have a strong home country "bias" and that makes investments like EEM an afterthought. It is not atypical to see a global equity portfolio reserve perhaps only 5-10% for emerging markets exposure.

That in itself provides a lower bar for profit potential, particularly when emerging market countries finally grow into their potential. That can take years or decades, which is why legendary EM investors like John Templeton, Mark Mobius, Charles Brandes and others are not household names like Warren Buffett, Ray Dalio and Peter Lynch, who have been US-focused.

Sector exposure for the MSCI EM Index, and thus for EEM, tends to shift over time. This is due to valuation changes, price appreciation/declines across sectors, and a range of other factors. However, one thing that has been consistent over time is that, similar to more developed non-US markets, EM lacks the tech giants that came to dominate the US stock market for years. Technology has always been one of the top 3-5 sector allocations for EEM, but that allocation is usually closer to 10-20%, versus the 20-35% or more investors have seen at times in the US market.

While EEM's passive managers aim to have the ETF replicate that index's economic characteristics, like country weightings, average market capitalization size, sector diversification and earnings/revenue/cash flow valuation metrics, not every stock in the index needs to be held. This is known in the ETF world as "representative sampling." But with so many stocks, it has been easy to use this approach to get the desired index tracking over time.

Emerging markets do not attract nearly the attention from long-term investors as US stocks and stocks of more developed nations do. Here's a chart of EEM's first 22 years of asset growth. It is a fraction of its iShares cousin ETFs whose mandate is to invest in the bigger countries of Europe and the Far East (the "EAFE" index). And it pales in comparison to the S&P 500's popularity.

Emerging markets tend not to be an afterthought but a lower-sized position in many US investors' portfolios. That said, there are times when EEM can be very competitive versus other equity market segments.

Using this snapshot from September 2025 as a rough indicator, we can discern a few general characteristics regarding EEM's typical holdings structure. This is a point in time but is fairly indicative of how EM stocks tend to come in versus the US market. The bottom line here: dividend yield is not too far off the US market, but in times of extreme US stock overvaluation, EEM may offer a modest dividend yield premium. Earnings and other classic valuation ratios did tend to be higher for EM stocks than US stocks, but when the US dominates for years, that relationship can flip.

EEM's first two decades can be neatly divided into two distinct performance periods. It was the global performance leader during the early part of the 21st century and then consistently underperformed both the US and developed non-US markets for most of the rest of that period.

As shown below, EEM can stay below its peak value for long periods of time. This has as much to do with changing sentiment over time as anything else. Investors go through periods in which their focus is on name-brand US icons, excluding nearly everything else. That impacts EEM negatively.

There have been and likely will continue to be times when, like small-cap stocks, EEM can be considered smaller, growthier, yet dramatically discounted to the big markets. Patience is required, as the market cycles here are very long. But they can also be quite fruitful.

Another criticism of EEM could be its continued high weighting to China-focused equity markets. This point in time (2025) shows that China and Taiwan (closely tied markets) made up about half of EEM's total exposure.

Furthermore, India and South Korea comprised another 27%. That's a very concentrated country allocation. And while it is based on the same index methodology used over a long period of time, investors have to ultimately decide when seeking exposure in this part of the world: do they want the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, or do they want to move more in the direction of less dependence on China and the other "Asia major" countries.

To that extent, other ETFs exist that target emerging Europe, smaller emerging Asian countries, and Latin America. In addition, there are over 50 single-country ETFs, most of which have existed since the 1990s.

The bottom line: it's a big, wide world of equity investing out there. EEM offers one of the most popular and streamlined routes to invest in emerging market stocks. Not the only one, but certainly the original one.

This article answers these three main questions about EEM:

  • How is EEM constructed?
  • Are there country concentration concerns with an investment in EEM?
  • Does EEM provide diversification on a sector and country basis?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Preformed Line Products: Overhanging Valuation To Catch Up With Share-Price Gains
data.description:
Intro

Our most recent commentary on Preformed Line Products Company (NASDAQ:PLPC) was in May of this year, when we reiterated our 'Hold' rating for the telecommunications and underground network-related outfit. The principal reasons for our cautious stance at the time were PLPC's overhanging valuation, as well as its subpar return on capital (6.86% in May of this year). At the time, PLPC was just off the back of reporting its Q1 earnings, where a strong performance in the PLP-USA segment drove company-wide sales to $148.5 million and GAAP income to $11.5 million for the quarter. However, as stated, we believed that the company's growth rates at the time did not justify the valuation on offer (especially concerning PLPC's revenues), which is why we maintained our cautious recommendation.

Consolidation in the stock took place until the release of PLPC's second-quarter earnings, which were released on 7/30/2025. Here, sentiment turned bullish once more, with revenues (buoyed once more by PLP-USA gains) growing by over 22% ($169.6 million), resulting in GAAP profit of $12.7 million for the quarter (35% year-over-year growth). Shares as a result are up close to 40% since our May commentary on 5/7/2025.

Valuations aside, a company's earnings growth curve remains the chief instigator of sustained share-price gains. To this point, taking into account that many of PLPC's international operations are now growing, investors believe the company's current growth path is sufficient to justify the overhanging valuation.

Although PLPC's manufacturing base in the US shields the company from Trump tariffs in one way, higher prices are coming for PLPC's customers due to increases in certain commodities, which the company needs to source. Therefore, through passing on price increases as well as ongoing value-added acquisitions, PLPC is attempting to stay ahead of the curve, as the Executive Chairman pointed out post the Q2 earnings release.

Our strong commitment to USA manufacturing provides a competitive advantage in the current high-tariff environment, but we will incur certain cost increases related to key commodity inputs necessary for our USA production process, primarily due to Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs. Through strong execution, we have managed the tariff impact well. We will continue to actively mitigate the impact of higher costs caused by tariffs and other global commodity cost increases by implementing targeted selling price increases and continued focus on cost containment and supply chain strategies. I am also happy to welcome newly acquired JAP Telecom to the PLP family, and I am confident in the contributions they will make to our communications product portfolio.

Intermediate Technical Chart

From a technical standpoint, we see that PLPC stock is either primed to break out above the 2023 lows or, indeed, a topping formation may be beginning to take hold. Although shares recently rallied above the 2023 highs, it is still too early to state whether a breakout has taken place in earnest. Therefore, when we have setups such as the following, we like to study under-reported metrics such as return on equity and free cash flow (as opposed to revenues and earnings) to gauge the fundamental strength of the stock. Remember, when ascertaining whether to buy a potential breakout, we are looking for these ratios or growth trends to be ahead of something like the PLPC's 5-year averages, which you feel is mandatory for this company, given as mentioned, its overhanging valuation

Below-Average Return On Equity

Earnings growth may be king on Wall Street, but many times investors fail to go under the hood to see how this growth is being created and whether it is sustainable. Earnings, for example, can grow if a company aggressively buys back its own stock or cuts its expenses, which many times are often temporary measures that eventually come to an end.

Return on equity measures the bottom-line profitability of PLPC (GAAP Profit) as a percentage of its equity. It is a key metric because immediately, we can see whether the company's assets are creating above-average value for shareholders or below-average.

To the above point, the chart below is revealing in that we see a 262% move in PLPC stock over the past 5 years has not coincided with strong growth trends in GAAP profit growth or ROE growth. In fact, although net income has grown by 29%+ over the past 5 years, trailing ROE of 9.67% comes in well below the stock's corresponding 5-year ROE average of 12.96%.

What the above trends demonstrate is that while PLPC has become a much bigger company in recent years with respect to the growth of its revenues/earnings, assets, and equity, invested capital in recent times has not delivered the same returns that we have been accustomed to in this company. This means management may feel it will have to invest more of its retained earnings to maintain present growth rates over time.

Free Cash Flow

Sometimes, a company can mask an above-average valuation by generating strong free cash flow, providing the fuel for future growth. When it boils down to it, free cash flow is all that matters as PLPC (with this cash) can continue investing organically and inorganically, pay a dividend, buy back stock, and reduce its leverage over time, all things remaining equal.

However, even though PLPC's GAAP profits have risen since mid-2024, free cash flow, as we see below, has been on a steady decline since late 2023. The company's trailing free cash-flow print currently comes in at $8.07 a share.

This means PLPC's trailing free cash-flow multiple comes in at approximately 24, again demonstrating that PLPC by historic multiples remains overvalued.

Conclusion

To sum up, although PLPC may look like it is primed to register a technical breakout, the company's under-average ROE and declining free cash flow demonstrate to us that consolidation is more likely than anything else. There just doesn't seem to be enough ammunition to fuel a sustained move higher. Maintaining 'Hold' rating. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Funding Stress Emerges As Liquidity Runs Dry
data.description:
With the reverse repo facility nearly drained and the Treasury refilling its cash pile, liquidity conditions have tightened, driving overnight funding rates higher and reserve balances at the Fed lower.

The TGA has reached approximately $670 billion as of September 11 and is still on pace to reach $850 billion by the end of September. Monday, September 15, is a tax day, and that should accelerate the advance in the TGA, pressuring the reserve lower. Meanwhile, the reverse repo facility has fallen below $30 billion and is no longer a source of liquidity.

Reserves Dropping

As a result of the rising TGA and the reverse repo facility now being drained, the reserve balance has been pushed lower, currently standing at $3.15 trillion. Reserves should continue to decline and are currently on pace to reach around $2.95 trillion by the end of September and potentially lower depending on the type of activity witnessed in the reserve repo facility for quarter-end.

Typically, around month-end and quarter-end, we see an increased usage of the reverse repo facility, as banks look to clear up their balance sheets. The end of September will mark a quarter end, and it is possible that usage could see the reverse repo facility exceed $100 billion.

Rising Borrowing Costs

The effects of falling reserves and the draining of excess liquidity are having an impact on the overnight funding markets, which has resulted in the secured overnight funding rate rising to 4.41%. This increase has also led to the 30-day moving average rate rising to 4.37%. That is actually the highest the average rate has been since the end of January.

This has pushed SOFR 8 bps above the effective funds rate. Overall, this would suggest that liquidity in the market is starting to feel the strain of the Fed balance sheet contraction. While rates have risen, we have not seen activity in the Fed's standing repo facility trigger consistently yet. They would need to start trading around the 4.5% level to really start seeing usage of this facility. That is where the Fed would become a provider of liquidity.

The activity in equity repo financing has fallen over the past few weeks, and this could be directly tied to the increasing overnight borrowing costs. Activity in equity repo financing has a pretty strong relationship with movements and changes in the stock market over time. This is one liquidity source that may continue to struggle if liquidity remains tight.

Fed Cuts Won't Help

Even when the Fed cuts the rate next week, it may not help much because the cost to borrow overnight is likely to remain elevated versus the prevailing Fed Funds rate. The market is already pricing in lower overnight rates following the Fed Funds rate cut, and right now, 2-week swaps are trading around 4.19%, which would be 9 bps higher than the midpoint of the Fed Funds rate range, which would likely be 4.08%. The current overnight swap trades around 4.43%, 10 bps higher than the effective funds rates.

Even once the TGA is filled, the Fed will still have quantitative tightening running in the background, and that will still work to drain reserves. At some point, the Fed will need to stop QT in the not-too-distant future. However, because Treasuries will continue to mature and roll off, it will force the Fed to start buying Treasury bills to keep the balance sheet stable.

This is not to imply that the Fed would be conducting QE, because the balance sheet should not be expanding under this exercise, even though it may first be thought of as such, which could prove to be very messy.
